Zycus will then present a Source-to-Pay demo, using Watchtower's language and vendor examples to ensure relevancy. Benjamin Rainsford is the key point of contact for coordinating this.
opp.ais_why:
• Watchtower's transformation needs center on 84 branches across 84 countries, multi-language support (including Croatia and Bulgaria), CLM with clause libraries and metadata extraction, and integration with 80+ ERP instances. AI is a capability they observed and may build themselves, not a top driver for buying from Zycus.
• The team did acknowledge the Merlin Intake and agentic sourcing demos favorably, praising clarity and the value-added analytics and AI capabilities. But the binding decision criterion is build-versus-buy on AI, which keeps this firmly in AI Resistant. Confidence is High because the buyer stated the build intent directly.

buyer.account_name: Watchtower Bible Tract Society of New York

items.quote: Sam 2026-04-09: 'we'll talk to our boss later today or later this week'

items.area: No quantified ROI / savings model on record
Homegrown-ERP integration not proven to the IT team
items.quote: Sam 2026-01-07: 'the biggest obstacle for us is integration into our own ERP'

metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated clear qualitative pain, roughly 10,000 contracts globally managed across hundreds of entities, an ERP contract 'black hole', and manual decentralized processes (Sam and Ben, Avoma discovery 2025-07-01 and demo 2025-08-04), plus a centralization program spanning 84 branches in 84 countries. But no savings, cycle-time, or ROI number was ever quantified. Jacob Blane flagged budget sensitivity on 2025-09-26: costs are 'harder to justify because personnel are volunteers.' Zycus offered a full ROI business case but never closed the loop with Watchtower's spend data, so the value stayed qualitative and lost to status-quo inertia; quantifying it is the top move.

champion.narrative: Benjamin Rainsford (Procurement Manager, Decision Maker per Contact Role) was the original champion who organized the demo and drove engagement Jul-Oct 2025, but he departed the company around Nov 2025. Sam Langsjo took over as the active advocate: he praised Zycus as 'by far the best' vendor, escalated the pilot internally, and pushed buy-in Feb-Apr 2026. What developed is genuine preference for Zycus; what is missing is authority and access to the budget owner. Sam admitted on 2026-04-09 that 'the hardest time with the business is it's not the business, it's just the internal stakeholders. A lot of good ideas get stopped because of that.' He is a coach without power, and engagement has cooled since 2026-04-09 with no reply to the 2026-06-18 check-in.

competition.narrative: The decisive competitor is the status quo, the existing ERP-based contract management the Ramapo contract stakeholders are 'happy with' without Zycus's added features (Sam, 2026-04-09), reinforced by an internal build option (five AI use cases being developed in-house, 2026-01-07). Among named external vendors (Coupa, GEP Worldwide, SAP Ariba), Zycus is the buyer-stated front-runner: Sam said on 2026-04-20 that of four vendors 'we are top choice given demo quality and CLM tools,' so the external field is a low current threat. We win only by defeating do-nothing, reframing value as the cost of manual contract management and tail-spend leakage against the pushed close date, delivered to the budget owner.

identify_pain.narrative: The confirmed pain is decentralized, manual contract and procurement management across 84 branches with no standardized supplier risk system and legal-heavy manual contracting (Grace and Ben framed this on 2025-08-04). Sam confirmed the CLM and tail-spend opportunity, and Jacob on 2025-09-26 noted a large upcoming construction project that will nearly double US spend, exposing tail-spend that autonomous negotiation could address. The inferred but unowned tension is that the Ramapo contract team is satisfied with the current ERP workflow, so the pain is real at the transformation level but not felt as urgent by the stakeholders who must approve.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics were never reached. An NDA is in place (Mike referenced 'we are under NDA' on 2025-09-26), and Zycus proposed a fixed-fee implementation with a scoping session, but no MSA/SOW, legal, procurement, or InfoSec track ever opened because the deal never cleared internal justification. A homegrown-ERP integration deep-dive was floated as a separate 'prove the how' session (Mike, 2026-01-07) but not scheduled. Visibility would come only after the buyer commits to a pilot; there is no contract-expiration forcing function on record.

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer was never identified or engaged in twelve months. Sam Langsjo and Nico are mid-level procurement contacts without budget authority; on 2026-04-09 Sam said 'we'll talk to our boss later today or later this week' about whether to readdress the pilot, but that senior leader was never named to Zycus and never joined a call. Jacob Blane (Strategic Account Director, Decision Maker per Contact Role) engaged on commercials on 2025-09-26 ('price on par with expectations') but did not present as the final budget owner. The evidence shows budget authority sits above Sam with an unnamed senior leader; reaching that person is the single highest-leverage gap to close.

decision_process.narrative: The process the buyer is actually running is an internal build-versus-buy deliberation gated by stakeholder buy-in, not a formal vendor bake-off. Reading the call sequence, Zycus ran discovery (2025-07-01), a broad demo with the CD/IT team (2025-08-04), a budget/pricing session (2025-09-26), a relaunch with new leads Sam and Nico (2026-01-07), and a pilot-scoping call (2026-04-09) where the buyer reported the internal stakeholders would not approve the move. Sam owns internal advocacy but must clear his boss and the Ramapo contract stakeholders. The approval chain runs Sam/Nico -> their manager (budget owner) -> contract stakeholders, and it stalled at that manager gate.

decision_criteria.narrative: Criteria are well understood from the call sequence: a modular source-to-contract scope (supplier management, contracts/CLM, sourcing), integration with Watchtower's homegrown ERP, multi-language and multi-country support for the Spain-led centralization, and AI/Merlin intake usability in Microsoft Teams for field users (Nico and Derek, Avoma 2026-01-07 and demo 2025-08-04). Derek probed the AI setup depth and per-branch policy configuration heavily on 2025-08-04. The binding criterion, however, is build-versus-buy on AI: the team is developing five use cases internally and weighs whether to build rather than buy, which the negative AIS reflects.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.

blocks.title: What moves it forward

cro_panel.header: Watchtower Bible Tract Society of New York — Formal Evaluation · $280K · closes 17 Sep

commentary.deal_risk: Risk 17 is driven by Verdict 'Off Track' — weak forward intent. Only observed negatives count, so these are real warning signs, not gaps.
commentary.win_position: Win 28 is carried by momentum 55 vs stage-expected 52; competitive strength +0.20 (weight 15). Offsetting it: differentiation strength -0.40 (weight 20).
commentary.deal_momentum: Momentum 55 (moving forward): Next Step carries 52 dated milestone(s) — actively worked. Silence alone was not treated as backward motion.
commentary.evidence_coverage: Partial Read: 4 of 7 evidence dimensions seen (engagement, stage integrity, exec and champion, forward motion). It labels how much of the picture we have, never the primary scores.
commentary.customer_commitment: Commitment sits near the earned-from-zero floor: little observable customer investment surfaced. Commitment is earned, so a low value is honest, not a penalty.
commentary.forecast_confidence: Forecast confidence 12 is anchored on the stage win (28), nudged by commitment 8 and momentum 55, and dragged by risk 17. Read is Partial Read — a confidence flag (we know less), not a haircut on the score.

contributions.evidence: Next Step carries 52 dated milestone(s) — actively worked.

Forecast cut Upside Key Deal -> Pipeline on 2026-06-17
confidence_signals.summary: Buyer engagement has cooled to silence since 2026-04-09; the internal forecast/stage downgrade on 2026-06-17 confirms falling confidence on the Zycus side too.

north_star_verdict.math: Today 2026-07-03; close date pushed 2026-06-30 from 2026-07-29 to 2026-09-17 (76 days out). Deal open since 2025-06-23 (375 days). Last two-way buyer touch 2026-04-09 (85 days ago); rep check-in 2026-06-18 drew no buyer reply. Stage relabelled Shortlisted -> Formal Evaluation on 2026-06-17 and forecast cut Upside Key Deal -> Pipeline the same day, signalling an internal downgrade. Cold for 60+ days forces Off Track regardless of the extended date. Pace required: reach the budget owner and land a Watchtower-specific ROI case in the next 2-3 weeks or the pushed date slips again.
evidence: Sam Langsjo on 2026-04-09: 'the stakeholders cannot justify the move towards this at this point in time' and 'they're happy with how it works without the additional features that you have' (Avoma pilot connect 2026-04-09)
Rep-initiated check-in 2026-06-18 ('check-in message w/ Sam + Nico for any updates') with no buyer response (Next_Step__c)
Forecast category cut Upside Key Deal -> Pipeline and stage moved Shortlisted -> Formal Evaluation on 2026-06-17, and close date pushed to 2026-09-17 on 2026-06-30 (OpportunityFieldHistory)
Economic buyer (Sam and Nico's boss) never brought into any Zycus conversation across the 12-month cycle despite the pilot being escalated internally (Avoma 2026-04-09)
No Watchtower-specific ROI business case ever delivered despite the buyer's need and Zycus's standing offer (Avoma budget call 2025-09-26; Avoma 2026-04-09)
north_star_verdict.headline: Deal is cold 85 days after the buyer's stakeholders rejected the pilot on 2026-04-09; economic buyer never engaged and the forecast was cut to Pipeline on 2026-06-17.

flags: The real competitor is do-nothing; reframe value as the cost of manual contract management and tail-spend, not features, to move the stakeholders.
The budget owner (Sam and Nico's boss) has never been engaged in 12 months; reaching that senior procurement leader is the deal's single biggest unlock.
Deal is cold 85 days and champion Sam has no authority; re-secure engagement and convert his preference into an executive introduction before the pushed date slips again.
best_practice_check.summary: Win-strategy levers for a cold, status-quo-blocked deal with an unmapped budget owner.

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is the status quo / do-nothing: on 2026-04-09 the Ramapo contract stakeholders were 'happy with how it works without the additional features,' backed by an internal build option for AI. Zycus is the buyer's stated top choice among the external field (Coupa, GEP, SAP Ariba per 2026-04-20), so those rivals are low current threats; the deal is lost or won on justifying the move against inertia, not against another vendor.

competitors.name: Status Quo / Existing ERP Contract Management
competitors.quote: Sam Langsjo 2026-04-09: 'they're happy with how it works without the additional features that you have'; 'the stakeholders cannot justify the move towards this at this point in time'
competitors.how_we_win: Quantify the cost of manual contract management and tail-spend leakage in a Watchtower-specific ROI case delivered to the budget owner, making inaction the expensive option.

competitors.name: Internal Build / In-House AI
competitors.quote: Nico 2026-01-07: 'we have our 5 use cases that we're working on internally with our own people from our computer departments. It's going a little bit slower'
competitors.how_we_win: Position Merlin agentic sourcing and intake as faster-to-value than a multi-year internal build, citing the 12-20 week implementation Jordan quoted.

competitors.name: GEP Worldwide
competitors.quote: Named in Salesforce Competitors__c; part of the 4-vendor evaluation where Sam said Zycus is 'top choice given demo quality and CLM tools' (Next_Step__c 2026-04-20)
competitors.how_we_win: Hold the demo-quality and CLM lead the buyer already acknowledged; keep Jordan's solution depth in front of the committee.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: Named in Salesforce Competitors__c; part of the 4-vendor evaluation, Zycus stated top choice
competitors.how_we_win: Lead with modular source-to-contract fit and AI-embedded value the buyer praised over Coupa.

competitors.name: SAP Ariba
competitors.quote: Named in Salesforce Competitors__c; part of the 4-vendor evaluation, Zycus stated top choice
competitors.how_we_win: Contrast Zycus's native ERP integration comfort and Teams-based Merlin intake usability against Ariba complexity.
